text: I'd like to talk in this video about a suturing technique we use for robotic ventral hernia while sewing in mesh. I like to call it the sewing machine technique. Others have called it the dolphin technique. But in either event, the needle goes in and comes right back out of the mesh, and we're going to go in a straight line rather than over the edge. But as you can see, we often start out in our learning curve sewing kind of like you just saw there, with a whole lot of different grabs on the needle. So if you actually count them here, you'll see that there's one here and then we're gonna take our other hand and kind of pull it all the way through and then take two or three more times to reposition it in the hand that's sewing.

The surgeon demonstrates a suturing technique by keeping the needle straight and using the non-dominant hand to pull it straight out of the tissue. The needle is unwound and handed back to the dominant hand, which is on the left, to continue sewing. The non-dominant hand pulls the needle all the way through, allowing the dominant hand to grab it about three-quarters of the way back for efficient needle passage.

So there you have it suturing the mesh made simple, at least a little more simple. And if you'd like any more information on this or other robotic techniques and things, you can visit clarkgerhart.com.
